parentQuestion: A 70-year-old man with chronic kidney disease has persistently high blood calcium levels despite dietary calcium restriction. His bones are becoming progressively weaker and X-rays show 'moth-eaten' appearance. Which cells are being OVERSTIMULATED by the hormone his kidneys can no longer properly regulate?

scaffolds.q: Bone serves as a storage reservoir for calcium.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! About 99% of body calcium is stored in bones. It can be released when blood calcium drops.

scaffolds.q: The suffix '-clast' means to break or destroy.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Osteoclast = bone breaker. Iconoclast = image/icon breaker. Clasts destroy things.

scaffolds.q: OSTEOCLASTS are cells that BREAK DOWN bone.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! They resorb bone matrix, releasing calcium into the bloodstream.

scaffolds.q: Parathyroid hormone (PTH) stimulates osteoclasts to release calcium from bone.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! When blood calcium is low, PTH tells osteoclasts to break down bone and release calcium.

scaffolds.q: Kidneys normally help regulate PTH and calcium levels.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Kidneys activate vitamin D and excrete phosphate - both crucial for calcium balance.

scaffolds.q: In kidney disease, PTH can become chronically elevated (hyperparathyroidism).
scaffolds.explain: Correct! The feedback loop breaks down, leading to persistent PTH secretion.

scaffolds.q: Chronically high PTH overstimulates osteoclasts, dissolving bone.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! The bones are literally being eaten away to maintain blood calcium. The 'moth-eaten' appearance on X-ray shows this.

scaffolds.q: In this patient, OSTEOCLASTS are being overstimulated by excess PTH.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! The kidney can't regulate PTH properly, so osteoclasts keep breaking down bone.

scaffolds.q: The answer is OSTEOCLASTS - they're breaking down bone due to uncontrolled PTH.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Not osteoblasts (they build), not osteocytes (they maintain). Osteoclasts are the bone destroyers responding to PTH.
